title_en: Marriage and Divorce: The Quranic Framework
content_en:
Al-Tahawi's Quranic treatment of marriage law centers on the prohibitions listed in Surah al-Nisa' (4:22-24). Verse 22 prohibits marrying women whom one's father married ('what your fathers married of women'), an eternal prohibition with no conditions attached. Verses 23-24 then list the permanently prohibited degrees of kinship and the temporarily or conditionally prohibited women. Al-Tahawi derives from verse 24's reference to 'what your right hands possess' (ma malakat aymanukum) the ruling on marriage or concubinage with slave women: the verse permits this subject to the conditions established by the Sunnah, and al-Tahawi carefully notes the Hanafi position that a free Muslim man may marry a free Jewish or Christian woman (ahl al-kitab) based on verse 5 of al-Ma'idah, while marriage to a polytheist (mushrika) is prohibited by Surah al-Baqarah (2:221).

The Quranic basis for the rules of divorce is found primarily in Surah al-Baqarah (2:226-232) and Surah al-Talaq (65:1-7). Al-Tahawi derives from Surah al-Baqarah (2:229), 'Divorce may be pronounced twice, then keep with kindness or release with good treatment,' the ruling that two revocable divorces do not dissolve the marriage permanently, allowing the husband to revoke within the iddah and return to the wife without a new contract. The third pronunciation, by contrast, makes the dissolution permanent, and the Quranic verse is read as establishing this three-stage structure. The verse 'until she marries another husband' (2:230) is particularly important to al-Tahawi as evidence that the Quran addresses the woman as a party to the contract, supporting the Hanafi position that an adult woman may contract her own marriage.

The phrase 'touching women' in the Quranic divorce passages refers to consummation, and al-Tahawi derives from Surah al-Ahzab (33:49) an important ruling on the iddah: 'O you who believe, when you marry believing women and then divorce them before you have touched them, there is no waiting period for you to count against them.' This verse establishes that an unConsummated marriage, when dissolved, requires no iddah from the wife, and the full mahr is due if it was specified (half if not specified with the other half waived). Al-Tahawi's careful reading of this verse and its interaction with the general waiting period rulings of Surah al-Baqarah illustrates how the ahkam al-Quran method requires the scholar to read all relevant verses together before deriving a ruling.

The rules on the iddah and its financial implications are drawn primarily from Surah al-Talaq (65:1-7). Al-Tahawi derives from verse 1 ('Do not expel them from their homes, nor should they leave') the Hanafi ruling that a divorced woman must remain in the marital home during the iddah, and her husband must maintain her there. From verse 6 ('If they are pregnant, spend on them until they deliver their burden, and if they nurse for you, give them their wages') al-Tahawi derives the distinct Hanafi rulings on the maintenance of a pregnant woman after irrevocable divorce and the wages of a wet-nurse who is the child's own mother. This integrated reading of the Quranic text serves as the foundation for the comprehensive Hanafi law of marital dissolution and its consequences.
content_id:
Pembahasan Al-Quran al-Thahawi tentang hukum pernikahan berpusat pada larangan-larangan yang tercantum dalam Surah al-Nisa' (4:22-24). Ayat 22 melarang menikahi perempuan-perempuan yang telah dinikahi ayah seseorang ('apa yang telah dinikahi bapak-bapakmu dari perempuan-perempuan'), sebuah larangan abadi tanpa syarat yang melekat. Ayat 23-24 kemudian mendaftar derajat-derajat kekerabatan yang dilarang secara permanen dan perempuan-perempuan yang dilarang secara sementara atau bersyarat. Al-Thahawi menurunkan dari referensi ayat 24 tentang 'apa yang dimiliki tangan kananmu' (ma malakat aymanukum) hukum tentang pernikahan atau kepemilikan budak perempuan: ayat tersebut mengizinkan ini tunduk pada syarat-syarat yang ditetapkan oleh Sunnah, dan al-Thahawi dengan cermat mencatat posisi Hanafi bahwa seorang laki-laki Muslim merdeka boleh menikahi perempuan Yahudi atau Nasrani merdeka (ahli kitab) berdasarkan ayat 5 al-Ma'idah, sementara pernikahan dengan perempuan musyrik dilarang oleh Surah al-Baqarah (2:221).

Dasar Al-Quran untuk aturan-aturan talak terutama ditemukan dalam Surah al-Baqarah (2:226-232) dan Surah al-Thalaq (65:1-7). Al-Thahawi menurunkan dari Surah al-Baqarah (2:229), 'Talak (yang dapat dirujuki) itu dua kali. Setelah itu boleh rujuk lagi dengan cara yang makruf atau menceraikan dengan cara yang baik,' hukum bahwa dua talak yang dapat dirujuki tidak membubarkan pernikahan secara permanen, memungkinkan suami untuk merujuk dalam masa iddah dan kembali kepada istri tanpa akad baru. Pengucapan ketiga, sebaliknya, membuat pembubaran itu permanen, dan ayat Al-Quran tersebut dibaca sebagai penetapan struktur tiga tahap ini. Ayat 'hingga ia menikah dengan suami yang lain' (2:230) sangat penting bagi al-Thahawi sebagai bukti bahwa Al-Quran menyapa perempuan sebagai pihak dalam akad, mendukung posisi Hanafi bahwa perempuan dewasa boleh mengakad pernikahannya sendiri.

Frasa 'menyentuh perempuan' dalam bagian-bagian talak Al-Quran merujuk pada konsumasi pernikahan, dan al-Thahawi menurunkan dari Surah al-Ahzab (33:49) hukum penting tentang iddah: "Wahai orang-orang yang beriman, apabila kamu menikahi perempuan-perempuan mukmin, kemudian kamu ceraikan mereka sebelum kamu mencampurinya maka tidak ada masa iddah bagi mereka yang perlu kamu perhitungkan." Ayat ini menetapkan bahwa pernikahan yang belum dikonsumasi, ketika dibubarkan, tidak memerlukan iddah dari istri, dan mahar penuh wajib diberikan jika telah ditentukan (setengah jika tidak ditentukan dengan setengahnya lagi dibebaskan). Pembacaan al-Thahawi yang cermat atas ayat ini dan interaksinya dengan hukum-hukum masa tunggu umum Surah al-Baqarah mengilustrasikan bagaimana metode ahkam al-Quran mengharuskan ulama untuk membaca semua ayat yang relevan bersama-sama sebelum menurunkan suatu hukum.

Aturan-aturan tentang iddah dan implikasi-implikasi keuangannya diambil terutama dari Surah al-Thalaq (65:1-7). Al-Thahawi menurunkan dari ayat 1 ('Jangan kamu keluarkan mereka dari rumah-rumah mereka dan janganlah mereka (diizinkan) keluar') hukum Hanafi bahwa perempuan yang ditalak harus tinggal di rumah pernikahan selama iddah, dan suaminya harus menafkahinya di sana. Dari ayat 6 ('Jika mereka (istri-istri yang sudah ditalak) itu sedang hamil, maka berikanlah kepada mereka nafkahnya hingga mereka bersalin, kemudian jika mereka menyusukan (anak-anak)mu untukmu maka berikanlah kepada mereka upahnya') al-Thahawi menurunkan hukum-hukum Hanafi yang khas tentang nafkah perempuan hamil setelah talak ba'in dan upah ibu menyusui yang merupakan ibu kandung dari anak tersebut. Pembacaan terpadu teks Al-Quran ini berfungsi sebagai fondasi hukum Hanafi yang komprehensif tentang pembubaran pernikahan dan konsekuensi-konsekuensinya.
content_ar: Al-Tahawi's القرآني treatment of marriage law centers on the prohibitions listed in السورة al-Nisa' (4:22-24). الآية 22 prohibits marrying women whom one's الأب married ('what your fathers married of women'), an eternal prohibition with no conditions attached. الآيات 23-24 then list the permanently prohibited degrees of kinship and the temporarily or conditionally prohibited women. Al-Tahawi derives from الآية 24's reference to 'what your الحق hands possess' (ma malakat aymanukum) the ruling on marriage or concubinage with slave women: the الآية permits this subject to the conditions established by the السنة, and al-Tahawi carefully notes the الحنفي position that a free مسلم man may marry a free Jewish or Christian woman (ahl al-kitab) based on الآية 5 of al-Ma'idah, بينما marriage to a polytheist (mushrika) is prohibited by السورة al-Baqarah (2:221). The القرآني basis for the rules of divorce is found primarily in السورة al-Baqarah (2:226-232) and السورة al-Talaq (65:1-7). Al-Tahawi derives from السورة al-Baqarah (2:229), 'Divorce may be pronounced twice, then keep with اللطف or release with الخير treatment,' the ruling that two revocable divorces do not dissolve the marriage permanently, allowing the الزوج to revoke within the iddah and return to the الزوجة without a new contract. The ثالثاً pronunciation, by contrast, makes the dissolution permanent, and the القرآني الآية is read as establishing this three-stage structure. The الآية 'حتى she marries another الزوج' (2:230) is particularly المهم to al-Tahawi as evidence that the القرآن الكريم addresses the woman as a party to the contract, supporting the الحنفي position that an adult woman may contract her own marriage. The phrase 'touching women' in the القرآني divorce passages refers to consummation, and al-Tahawi derives from السورة al-Ahzab (33:49) an المهم ruling on the iddah: 'O you who believe, عندما you marry believing women and then divorce them قبل you have touched them, there is no waiting period for you to count against them.' This الآية establishes that an unConsummated marriage, عندما dissolved, requires no iddah from the الزوجة, and the full mahr is due إذا it was specified (half إذا not specified with the other half waived). Al-Tahawi's careful reading of this الآية and its interaction with the general waiting period rulings of السورة al-Baqarah illustrates how the ahkam al-القرآن الكريم method requires the scholar to read all relevant الآيات together قبل deriving a ruling. The rules on the iddah and its financial implications are drawn primarily from السورة al-Talaq (65:1-7). Al-Tahawi derives from الآية 1 ('Do not expel them from their homes, nor should they leave') the الحنفي ruling that a divorced woman must remain in the marital home خلال the iddah, and her الزوج must maintain her there. From الآية 6 ('إذا they are pregnant, spend on them حتى they deliver their burden, and إذا they nurse for you, give them their wages') al-Tahawi derives the distinct H
